name: Strawman
description: Misrepresenting someone's argument to make it easier to attack.
impact: Derails rational discussion by attacking a distorted version of the argument.
argument_patterns: Original claim A -> exaggerated claim A' -> attack A'
Restating opponent's position in a more extreme way before criticizing
counter_questions: Can you restate my argument in a way I would agree with?
Where exactly did I say that?
Are we discussing the same claim?

name: False Cause
description: Assuming that because two things occur together, one causes the other.
impact: Leads to incorrect causal conclusions and poor policy or strategy choices.
argument_patterns: A happens before B -> therefore A caused B
A correlates with B -> therefore A causes B
counter_questions: What evidence shows causation rather than correlation?
What other factors might explain the relationship?

name: Slippery Slope
description: Arguing that a small step will inevitably lead to extreme consequences without sufficient evidence.
impact: Creates exaggerated fear and blocks reasonable decisions.
argument_patterns: A -> B -> C -> catastrophic Z
Small change framed as leading to societal collapse
counter_questions: What evidence supports each step?
Are there examples where A happened without leading to Z?

name: Ad Hominem
description: Attacking the person instead of addressing the argument.
impact: Shifts focus away from evaluating evidence.
trigger_phrases: You're not qualified to say that
argument_patterns: Person P makes argument A -> attack P -> conclude A is false
counter_questions: How does that relate to the argument itself?
Can we evaluate the claim independently of the person?
Would the argument be stronger or weaker if someone else made it?

name: Special Pleading
description: Applying standards or exceptions to your own claim while denying those standards to others.
impact: Breaks fair evaluation by shielding favored claims from scrutiny.
argument_patterns: General rule R for others -> exception for my claim without justification
Counterexample presented -> ad hoc exemption added
counter_questions: What principled reason justifies this exception?
Would you accept the same exception from the other side?

name: Loaded Question
description: Asking a question with a built-in assumption that forces a damaging answer.
impact: Derails discussion by presuming guilt or agreement.
argument_patterns: Question Q presupposes disputed claim P
Any direct answer implies acceptance of P
counter_questions: What assumption is built into this question?
Can we first establish whether that premise is true?

name: The Gambler's Fallacy
description: Believing past random outcomes make opposite outcomes more likely in independent events.
impact: Causes systematic errors in risk and betting decisions.
argument_patterns: Independent event repeats -> conclude reversal is now likely
Run of outcomes treated as causal pressure
counter_questions: Are these events statistically independent?
How do the base probabilities change from one trial to the next?

name: Bandwagon
description: Appealing to popularity as proof that something is true.
impact: Encourages herd thinking instead of evidence-based reasoning.
trigger_phrases: It's popular so it must be true
argument_patterns: Many people believe X -> therefore X is true
counter_questions: Does popularity make something correct?
What evidence supports this claim independently of its popularity?
Can you think of popular beliefs that turned out to be wrong?

name: Appeal to Authority
description: Using an authority's opinion as proof instead of presenting evidence.
impact: Discourages independent evaluation of claims.
argument_patterns: Authority A believes claim X -> therefore X is true
counter_questions: Is the authority qualified in this specific field?
What direct evidence supports the claim?
Do other equally qualified authorities disagree?

name: Composition
description: Assuming that what is true of parts must also be true of the whole.
impact: Produces invalid inferences in systems, teams, and statistics.
trigger_phrases: Each part is... so the whole is...
If members have property P, the group has P
argument_patterns: Part has property P -> whole has property P
Local trait generalized globally without evidence
counter_questions: Why should part-level properties transfer to the whole?
What evidence supports this at the whole-system level?
Can you think of cases where parts have a property but the whole does not?

name: No True Scotsman
description: Dismissing counterexamples by redefining a group to protect a universal claim.
impact: Prevents belief revision by moving standards after contradiction appears.
trigger_phrases: That doesn't count as a real example
argument_patterns: Claim: all X are Y -> counterexample X not Y -> redefine X to exclude case
counter_questions: Was this criterion defined before the counterexample appeared?
Are you revising the claim or just shielding it?

name: Genetic Fallacy
description: Judging a claim as true or false based on its origin rather than its merits.
impact: Substitutes provenance judgments for evidence quality.
trigger_phrases: It came from them, so it's wrong
That idea is from X, therefore unreliable
Given the source, we can ignore it
argument_patterns: Claim C originates from source S -> conclude C true/false because of S
counter_questions: What is the evidence for the claim itself?
Does the source determine truth value here?

name: Black-or-White (False Dilemma)
description: Presenting two options as the only possibilities when others exist.
impact: Oversimplifies complex decisions and polarizes discussion.
trigger_phrases: Either you're with us or against us
argument_patterns: Only A or B presented -> ignore alternatives C, D, E
counter_questions: Are there other possible options?
Why must these be the only two choices?

name: Begging the Question
description: The conclusion is assumed within the premise.
impact: Creates arguments without independent support.
argument_patterns: Claim A used as evidence for claim A
counter_questions: What independent evidence supports the claim?
Is the premise just restating the conclusion in different words?
Can we find separate evidence for each part of this argument?

name: Appeal to Nature
description: Assuming something is good because it is natural.
impact: Rejects beneficial interventions and accepts harmful 'natural' ones.
argument_patterns: X is natural -> therefore X is good
counter_questions: Does natural always mean better?
What evidence shows it is beneficial?
Can you name natural things that are harmful and artificial things that are helpful?

name: Anecdotal
description: Using isolated personal stories in place of broader, systematic evidence.
impact: Overweights vivid examples and ignores representative data.
argument_patterns: Single case cited -> general rule concluded
Personal testimony used to dismiss statistical studies
counter_questions: How representative is this example?
What do large-sample studies show?

name: The Texas Sharpshooter
description: Highlighting data clusters that fit a claim while ignoring the full pattern.
impact: Creates false confidence by manufacturing patterns from noise.
trigger_phrases: Look at these examples that prove it
If you focus on these points
argument_patterns: Data set D -> select favorable subset S -> infer broad conclusion C
Pattern identified after outcome known
counter_questions: What happens when we include all relevant data?
Was the hypothesis specified before looking at the data?

name: Middle Ground
description: Assuming the truth must lie between two opposing positions.
impact: Treats compromise as evidence, even when one side is simply wrong.
trigger_phrases: The truth is probably in the middle
Both sides are equally right and wrong
argument_patterns: Claim A vs claim B -> conclude midpoint M is true without evidence
counter_questions: Why should the midpoint be more accurate?
What independent evidence supports the compromise position?

name: Appeal to Emotion
description: Using emotional pressure in place of relevant evidence.
impact: Can override critical evaluation and lead to poor decisions.
trigger_phrases: If you cared, you would agree
argument_patterns: Evoke strong emotion E -> treat E as proof of claim C
counter_questions: What evidence supports this conclusion?
How would we evaluate this claim if emotion were lower?

name: Tu Quoque
description: Rejecting criticism by accusing the critic of similar behavior.
impact: Deflects from whether the original criticism is valid.
argument_patterns: Criticism of A -> respond with hypocrisy claim about critic -> dismiss criticism
counter_questions: Even if that is true, does it address the claim itself?
Can we evaluate the criticism on its own merits?

name: Personal Incredulity
description: Concluding a claim is false because it seems difficult to understand or imagine.
impact: Blocks learning about complex evidence and mechanisms.
trigger_phrases: I can't imagine how that could be true
That makes no sense to me
It's too weird to be real
argument_patterns: I don't understand X -> therefore X is false
counter_questions: Does my lack of understanding imply falsehood?
What do domain experts and evidence say?

name: Burden of Proof
description: Shifting responsibility to others to disprove a claim instead of supporting it.
impact: Allows unsupported assertions to persist without evidence.
argument_patterns: Claim C asserted without evidence -> demand disproof from others
counter_questions: What positive evidence supports the claim?
Why should non-disproof count as proof?
Who made the claim first, and what was their evidence?

name: Ambiguity
description: Using unclear or double-meaning language to imply unsupported conclusions.
impact: Confuses interpretation and permits misleading arguments.
argument_patterns: Term T used in sense S1 in premise and S2 in conclusion
Grammatical ambiguity exploited to mislead
counter_questions: Can we define this key term precisely?
Are we using the same meaning throughout?

name: The Fallacy Fallacy
description: Assuming a conclusion is false simply because the argument for it is fallacious.
impact: Confuses argument quality with truth value and can reject true claims.
trigger_phrases: That argument is fallacious, so the claim is false
You used a fallacy, therefore you're wrong
argument_patterns: Argument for claim C is invalid -> conclude not C
counter_questions: Even if the argument is flawed, what is the actual evidence for or against the claim?
Can the conclusion still be true for other reasons?
Am I conflating argument quality with truth of the conclusion?
